source: DPWH Blue Book, NSCP 2015, ASTM/PNS Standards, Industry Practice

rules.description: Architects must understand basic structural systems — you're the team leader

conditions.must_know: beam-column grid logic, load paths, slab types (one-way vs two-way), cantilever limits, expansion joints, seismic bracing concepts

rules.notes: You don't design the structure, but you must know when your design creates structural problems. A column-free 8m span is doable with post-tensioned slab but not with conventional. Know the cost of what you draw.

rules.description: Column grid should align with architectural layout — coordinate early
rules.notes: The #1 coordination failure in PH construction: architect draws rooms, structural engineer adds columns that land in doorways and corridors. Share floor plans with the structural engineer at schematic stage, not after construction docs are done.

rules.description: Understand rebar — standard Philippine sizes and concrete cover requirements

conditions.concrete_cover: 40mm for columns, 50mm for foundation, 25mm for slabs (interior)

rules.notes: You'll inspect rebar placement during CA. Know what correct looks like. Insufficient cover = corrosion in 10 years in PH climate. Spacing, lap lengths, stirrup hooks — learn to read structural details.

rules.description: Understand CHB (concrete hollow block) construction — the Filipino standard

conditions.class: Class A (10.34 MPa) for structural, Class B (6.90 MPa) for non-bearing

rules.notes: 90%+ of PH residential construction uses CHB. Know the difference between Class A and B — many contractors use Class B everywhere to save money. Check by visual inspection: Class A is denser, heavier, more uniform. Specify Class A in your specs.

rules.description: Concrete mix design — know the standard Philippine specs
rules.notes: Insist on concrete mix design from a testing lab for projects above PHP 2M. On smaller residential, specify sacks-per-cubic-meter ratio. Common error: contractors use excess water for workability — reduces strength by 20-30%. Slump test on-site.

rules.description: Curing is critical — concrete must be wet-cured for 7 days minimum
rules.notes: Most PH contractors strip formwork at 3 days and never cure. This costs 30% of concrete strength. Specify curing in your contract. Insist on it during CA. Curing compounds are acceptable alternative. This is the single cheapest quality improvement.

rules.description: Waterproofing — the most common construction defect in the Philippines

conditions.critical_areas: roof deck, toilet floors, balconies, below-grade walls, planter boxes, water tanks

rules.notes: 50%+ of PH building complaints are water leakage. Specify waterproofing membrane (not just paint-on) for all wet areas. Flood test every toilet and balcony for 24 hours before tiling. Experienced architects inspect waterproofing personally — they don't delegate it.

rules.description: Know current material prices — you can't design without budget awareness
rules.notes: Prices fluctuate 10-20% annually. New architects specify materials without knowing cost — then the client can't afford their design. Keep a running price list. Visit hardware stores quarterly. Know the difference between Iloilo, Manila, and Cebu pricing.

rules.description: Specify materials by performance, not brand — avoid liability and bias
rules.notes: Write 'porcelain floor tile, 600x600mm, PEI 4, water absorption <0.5%, or approved equal' — NOT 'Niro Granite GHP04.' Brand specs limit competition and can create liability. Use 'or approved equal' for everything. Let the contractor submit alternatives for your approval.

rules.description: Tropical material durability — know what survives PH climate

conditions.avoid: untreated mild steel (rusts in months), MDF in wet areas (swells), wallpaper in non-AC spaces (mold), carpet in humid zones
conditions.prefer: stainless steel 304/316 for hardware, marine-grade aluminum, fiber cement over gypsum for soffits, porcelain over ceramic for exteriors

rules.notes: PH climate (30°C, 80%+ RH, salt air near coast, typhoon rain) destroys materials that work fine in temperate countries. If you learned it from a US or European textbook, verify it works in tropical conditions.

rules.description: Window specifications — the most argued detail in PH residential
rules.notes: Clients fixate on windows. Specify precisely: frame material, glass type (clear/tinted/laminated/tempered), operation type, hardware, screen. For typhoon zones: tempered or laminated glass mandatory. Sliding windows leak air — casement/awning are superior for ventilation.

rules.description: Coordinate ceiling height with MEP — allow 300mm for ductwork and piping
rules.notes: 3000mm floor-to-floor with 150mm slab = 2850mm. Subtract 300mm MEP zone = 2550mm ceiling height. If you promised the client 2700mm ceiling, something has to give. Coordinate MEP routes at design development, not during construction.

rules.description: Electrical load computation — understand the basics even as an architect

conditions.residential_rule: 50-75 VA per sqm general lighting, plus 1500W per appliance circuit
conditions.common_mistake: undersized main breaker and wire gauge

rules.notes: You're not the PEE, but you should know: a 200sqm house needs ~100A service. AC units need dedicated circuits. Kitchen needs 20A circuits. If you design a house with 4 split-type ACs and the electrician sizes for 2, your client's breaker trips daily.

rules.description: Plumbing — drainage slope and vent requirements

conditions.vent_required: every fixture group needs a vent stack

rules.notes: Common PH construction error: no venting. Water drains slowly, gurgling sounds, sewer gas enters building. Architect should verify plumbing layout shows vent stacks — not just drains. Second-floor toilets directly above first-floor toilets saves money and problems.
